body.name: Nighthawk Napoletana
body.description: Wood-fired Neapolitan pizza with a blazing hot oven producing beautiful crusts. Family-friendly with a bar in the back. First spot you see off the ferry.

body.name: Tout de Suite Cafe
body.description: Cheerful neighborhood coffeehouse serving French and Cajun-inspired fare for breakfast and lunch. Local art on the walls, all for sale.

body.name: Congregation Coffee
body.description: New Orleans-based roaster with a cafe that feels like the neighborhood's living room. Great espresso and pastries.

body.name: Plume Algiers
body.description: Modern Indian cuisine from a chef with a fine dining background. Kaathi rolls, tandoori chicken, and creative cocktails in an intimate setting.

body.name: Barracuda
body.description: All-outdoor taco spot in a converted gas station. Try the chicken turmeric adobo or red chili pork. Make anything deluxe for a dollar.

body.name: The Old Point Bar
body.description: Corner bar with sidewalk seats, 16 draft beers, and some of the best live music in the city. A true neighborhood institution.

body.name: Crown & Anchor Pub
body.description: New Orleans' only authentic English pub. Enter through the blue phone booth into a world of imported ales, pub games, and a large patio.

body.name: The Little House
body.description: Small plates, cocktails, and wine in a cozy bottle shop setting. Relax in the breezy backyard garden.

body.name: Rice Vice
body.description: Intimate sake and cocktail bar. Reservations recommended for this unique neighborhood gem.

body.name: Dry Dock Cafe
body.description: Classic diner serving burgers, salads, cocktails, and homemade soft serve for lunch and dinner.

body.name: Appetite Repair Shop
body.description: Legendary neighborhood pop-up. Follow the Facebook group for the rotating menu. Venmo only. A true hidden gem.

body.name: Bargeboard Mercantile & Millshop
body.description: Vintage furniture, architectural salvage, local art, and repurposed fixtures from local craftsmen. Part retail store, part event space, part woodworking millshop.

body.name: Rosetree Blown Glass Studio
body.description: Artist Mark Rosenbaum's studio in a restored 1930s art deco theater. Watch glass being blown and 'blow your own' on select days.

body.name: Jazz Walk of Fame
body.description: Begins with a bronze statue of Louis Armstrong right off the ferry. Lampposts along the levee honor jazz legends like Sidney Bechet, Papa Celestin, and the Boswell Sisters.

body.name: Algiers Courthouse
body.description: Built in 1896 after the great fire, this Moorish-Romanesque landmark stands on the former site of the Duverje Plantation (1812). Oldest continuously used courthouse in Louisiana.

body.name: Historical Levee Plaques
body.description: Look down as you walk. Plaques in the pavement tell the story of each spot, from the enslaved Africans' holding area to Jean Lafitte's canal route to Barataria Bay.

body.name: Folk Art Zone & Blues Museum
body.description: Home of self-taught artist Charles Gillam. A colorful outdoor gallery and museum celebrating folk art and blues heritage.

body.name: Holy Name of Mary Church
body.description: Enormous Tudor Gothic cathedral with a clock tower and over 75 stained glass windows, several from the Meyer firm of Munich. The site was once used for voodoo dancing and was a Civil War prison camp.

body.name: 200 Block of Olivier St
body.description: A single block spanning 50 years of architectural styles: Greek Revival, Italianate, Eastlake, Queen Anne, and Creole Cottage. A living textbook of New Orleans building traditions.

body.name: Trinity Lutheran Church
body.description: The oldest surviving structure in Algiers. Originally faced Olivier Street before being moved in the 1890s. Beautiful stained glass and historic church bells in the grounds.

body.name: Martin Behrman School
body.description: The first public high school in Algiers. Spanish Revival Colonial style with decorative stonework, terracotta roof tiles, and an ornate bell tower.

body.name: Hubbell Library
body.description: The oldest public library building in New Orleans and one of only two surviving Carnegie libraries in the city. Named for Cita Dennis Hubbell who fought to keep it open.

body.name: 630 Pacific Ave
body.description: New Orleans' most famous shotgun house, featured on PBS Television's This Old House in 1991. All rooms arranged in a single row.
